lesson.name: Descartes: Method & Meditations - Lesson 1 — A Discourse on Method

meta.lessonObjective: Methodical doubt and the rules of reason.

tasks.chapterTitle: Introduction and the Nature of Reason
Reflections on Education and Early Inquiries
Project of Self-Reform and Methodological Slowliness
Anatomy and the Circulation of Blood

messages.content:
Lesson 1 — A Discourse on Method - Presentation26 Slides
The Equal Distribution of Reason
  • Good sense or reason is by nature equal in all men, so that the diversity of our opinions does not arise from some being endowed with a larger share than others.
  • The prime requisite is rightly to apply reason, for to be possessed of a vigorous mind is not enough.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the text, why does diversity of opinions arise among men?
options: Some men possess more reason than others
We conduct our thoughts along different ways and do not fix attention on the same objects
Reason is unequally distributed by nature
Education alone creates all differences
questions.explanation: Page 1 states diversity arises solely because we conduct thoughts along different ways.

questions.question: What was Descartes' first resolution in forming his method?
options: To accept probable opinions from the schools
Never to accept anything for true which he did not clearly know to be such
To follow the majority in all matters
To reject mathematics entirely
questions.explanation: Page 7 gives the first rule: never accept anything not clearly known to be true.

questions.question: Why did Descartes choose moderate opinions in his provisional morals?
options: They are always the most convenient and probably the best
They guarantee certain truth
They were commanded by his teachers
They align with ancient philosophers
questions.explanation: Page 7 states he chose the most moderate as most convenient and probably best.

questions.question: What does the text say about the ground of most opinions?
options: They rest on certain knowledge
They rest far more on custom and example
They come from innate ideas alone
They derive from divine revelation
questions.explanation: Page 5 concludes the ground is far more custom and example than certain knowledge.

questions.question: Why is the principle that clear and distinct conceptions are true certain, per the text?
options: Because the senses never deceive
Because God is a Perfect Being from whom all we possess is derived
Because many philosophers agree on it
Because it holds in dreams as well
questions.explanation: Page 11 states the principle is certain only because God exists and is perfect.

cards.front: What is the prime requisite for reason according to Descartes?
cards.back: Rightly to apply it, since a vigorous mind is not enough.

cards.front: What action did Descartes take with all opinions he had embraced?
cards.back: He resolved to sweep them wholly away to admit others after scrutiny of reason.

cards.front: What is the first rule of the method?
cards.back: Never to accept anything for true which he did not clearly know to be such.

cards.front: What is the second rule of the method?
cards.back: To divide each difficulty into as many parts as possible for adequate solution.

cards.front: What is the third rule of the method?
cards.back: To conduct thoughts in order from simplest to most complex objects.

cards.front: What is the fourth rule of the method?
cards.back: To make enumerations so complete that nothing is omitted.

cards.front: Why did Descartes not imitate the sceptics?
cards.back: His design was to find ground of assurance, not to doubt for its own sake.

cards.front: What ground did Descartes infer for most opinions?
cards.back: Far more custom and example than any certain knowledge.

cards.front: What assurance does the text say we lack without presupposing God?
cards.back: Assurance that waking thoughts are not false like those in dreams.

cards.front: Why is the principle of clear and distinct truth certain?
cards.back: Because God is a Perfect Being and all we possess is derived from him.

slides.title: The Equal Distribution of Reason
bullets: Good sense or reason is by nature equal in all men, so that the diversity of our opinions does not arise from some being endowed with a larger share than others.
The prime requisite is rightly to apply reason, for to be possessed of a vigorous mind is not enough.
Without a disciplined path to guide its use, this shared reason scatters into contradictory beliefs because each person follows private habits rather than a common order of examination.
The real difference among minds therefore appears only after we impose strict rules that compel us to doubt everything capable of being doubted and to accept solely what survives such scrutiny.
slides.notes: Opens Part I on the nature of reason.

slides.title: Conducting Thoughts Along Different Ways
bullets: Those who travel very slowly may yet make far greater progress if they keep always to the straight road than those who run and forsake it.
I have never fancied my mind more perfect than those of the generality, yet I formed a method from certain tracks found early in life.
By refusing every step that cannot be traced back to an indubitable starting point, the mind travels slowly yet never has to retrace its course after discovering an error.
The same ordinary faculties, when kept to this single ordered path, therefore reach truths that remain hidden from quicker minds which scatter their attention across unexamined opinions.

slides.title: The Need to Rebuild Foundations
bullets: I resolved at once to sweep away all opinions I had embraced up to that time so that I might admit others more correct after scrutiny of reason.
It is preposterous for a private individual to reform the sciences as a whole, yet reforming one's own opinions is feasible.

Once every inherited view has been cleared, the mind confronts an empty space that must be filled only by what survives the strictest test, so the first new beliefs carry the full weight of everything that will later rest upon them.

Because this labor belongs to one person at a time, the certainty finally reached remains private until it proves sturdy enough to be offered to others, creating the quiet risk that solitude may either protect truth or conceal an unnoticed flaw.
slides.notes: Transition to methodical doubt in Part II.

slides.title: The Example of Rebuilding a House
bullets: A private individual may take down his own house to erect it anew when foundations are insecure, and the same holds for opinions.
I firmly believed this way would better succeed in the conduct of life than building upon old foundations taken on trust in youth.

Once the insecure foundations have been cleared away, every opinion must be tested one by one against the natural light of reason, so that whatever survives can serve as a stone that will never shift again.

Yet the work of reconstruction cannot be completed in a single day, and therefore temporary rules of conduct must be adopted to guide action while certainty is still being secured, otherwise life itself would come to a standstill.

slides.title: Dangers of Precipitate Doubt
bullets: Those who with more than due confidence are precipitate in judgments and want patience for orderly thinking will lose themselves if they quit the beaten highway.
The single design to strip one's self of all past beliefs ought not to be taken by every one.
Without the steady guidance of ordered steps, total doubt leaves the mind adrift with no reliable way to rebuild what it has set aside, so everyday decisions become impossible.
Only those already practiced in patient reasoning can hope to replace discarded beliefs with clearer ones; everyone else risks exchanging workable convictions for nothing at all.

slides.title: Ground of Opinions in Custom
bullets: I was led to infer that the ground of our opinions is far more custom and example than any certain knowledge.
A plurality of suffrages is no guarantee of truth where discovery is difficult.

Once childhood habits settle into the mind they continue to steer judgment long after we suppose ourselves capable of independent thought, so that even our most confident assertions often echo the unexamined patterns of our earliest surroundings.

Because truth in difficult questions cannot be tallied like votes, reliance on shared opinion merely multiplies the same untested assumptions and leaves the solitary inquirer without any external authority to confirm or correct the path of methodical doubt.
slides.notes: Leads into need for methodical approach.

slides.title: The First Rule of the Method
bullets: I resolved never to accept anything for true which I did not clearly know to be such, avoiding precipitation and prejudice.
I would include nothing more in my judgments than what was so clearly and distinctly presented to the mind that I could have no occasion to doubt it.

This rule compels me to treat even long-held opinions as suspect until each has passed the test of perfect clarity, which means the entire structure of my former knowledge must be dismantled before any part of it can be restored.

The immediate consequence is a deliberate period of radical uncertainty that feels like intellectual ruin, yet only this disciplined withholding of assent can prevent a single unnoticed prejudice from toppling whatever I later accept as certain.
slides.notes: Core of methodical doubt from Part II.

slides.title: The Second Rule of the Method
bullets: I would divide each of the difficulties under examination into as many parts as possible and as might be required for its adequate solution.
This division allows each truth discovered to serve as a rule for subsequent ones.

By isolating each element in turn the mind escapes the fog that arises when many obscure notions press upon it simultaneously, so that attention can fix itself upon one clear point until its truth stands revealed.

This stepwise ascent from the simplest components to the more intricate ones ensures that no later conclusion can undermine an earlier certainty, for every new rule rests only upon what has already been secured beyond doubt.

slides.title: The Third Rule of the Method
bullets: I would conduct my thoughts in order, commencing with the simplest and easiest objects to know, and ascending little by little to the knowledge of the most complex.
I would suppose an order even among those objects which do not follow a natural sequence.
This ordered ascent ensures that each new truth rests securely upon those already secured, so that no later discovery can undermine the earlier ones and the whole edifice of knowledge remains stable.

Where nature presents only a disordered mass of appearances, the mind's self-imposed sequence turns confusion into clarity by isolating the simplest elements first and then reassembling them step by step.

slides.title: The Fourth Rule of the Method
bullets: I would make enumerations so complete and reviews so general that I might be assured that nothing was omitted.
The method teaches adherence to true order and exact enumeration of all conditions.
Such exhaustive enumeration turns the mind’s initial uncertainty into a controlled process where every possible link in a chain of reasoning must be inspected before certainty can be claimed.

Yet the demand for total review also exposes a practical limit of finite intellects, compelling the thinker to rely on repeated general surveys that gradually eliminate the lingering possibility of unnoticed error.

slides.title: Borrowing from Geometry and Algebra
bullets: I believed I could borrow all that was best both in geometrical analysis and in algebra, and correct all the defects of the one by help of the other.
Expressing thoughts by the briefest characters allows retention in memory of an aggregate of many.

This fusion turns the mind into an instrument capable of tracing long chains of reasoning without losing a single link, so that truths once scattered across many pages become visible at a single glance.
Yet the very brevity of the symbols can tempt us to treat thought as mere manipulation, risking the loss of the living insight that first gave those symbols meaning.

slides.title: Commencing with Simplest Truths
bullets: I commenced with the simplest and most general truths, so that each truth discovered was a rule available in the discovery of subsequent ones.
The child instructed in elements of arithmetic may be assured he has found the sum according to rule.
By anchoring each new insight to these elementary certainties I ensure that error cannot multiply unnoticed, for any flaw would already have surfaced at the first step.
This patient ascent therefore converts the mind’s initial poverty of knowledge into a steadily widening domain where every later truth inherits the same unshakable warrant.

slides.title: Applying the Method Beyond Mathematics
bullets: I hoped from not having restricted this method to any particular matter to apply it to the difficulties of the other sciences with no less success.
I reserved some hours expressly devoted to the employment of the method in the solution of mathematical difficulties and questions from other sciences rendered almost mathematical.

This extension means treating the motions of bodies or the functions of living things as chains of simple relations that can be ordered and measured, so that what once passed for opinion becomes a chain of evident steps.

The same discipline therefore carries a quiet demand that every other inquiry first be stripped of its borrowed authority and rebuilt from what the mind can grasp clearly, or else remain outside the reach of certainty.

slides.title: The Design to Find Assurance
bullets: My design was singly to find ground of assurance and cast aside the loose earth and sand that I might reach the rock or the clay.
I endeavored to discover the falsehood or incertitude of propositions not by feeble conjectures but by clear and certain reasonings.

This search for solid ground forces me to treat every inherited opinion as suspect until it has passed through the fire of methodical examination, lest an unnoticed flaw bring the whole edifice down later.

The refusal to accept anything less than clear reasonings therefore opens a temporary emptiness in which familiar certainties vanish, yet it also creates the only path by which genuine assurance can ever be regained.

slides.title: Nothing So Doubtful Without Conclusion
bullets: I met with nothing so doubtful as not to yield some conclusion of adequate certainty, although this were merely the inference that the matter contained nothing certain.
In destroying ill-founded opinions I made observations and acquired experience for establishing more certain ones.
Even the most barren doubt therefore leaves behind a precise boundary, showing the understanding exactly where it must begin its search for what cannot be shaken.
The labor of rejection thus becomes an apprenticeship in judgment, training the mind to recognize the difference between what merely seems evident and what truly withstands scrutiny.

slides.title: Suspending Judgment While Living
bullets: It is necessary to be furnished with some other house in which we may live commodiously during rebuilding operations.
I formed a provisory code of morals so that I might not remain irresolute in actions while reason compelled suspension of judgment.
Such a temporary code therefore lets one meet ordinary obligations and move through the world without waiting for every last certainty to arrive first.
At the same time it keeps the thinker inwardly free to revise those very rules once firmer principles come into view, so that action never hardens into dogma.

slides.title: Obeying Moderate Opinions
bullets: I chose always the most moderate opinions as much because these are the most convenient for practice and probably the best, as that in error I might be at less distance from the truth.
I regulated conduct according to the most moderate opinions farthest removed from extremes adopted with general consent of the judicious.
This strategy let me act with confidence in daily affairs while the work of radical doubt continued undisturbed in private thought, preventing endless hesitation from paralyzing ordinary life.
It also created a quiet safeguard against the costly reversals that extreme positions often demand once stronger reasons emerge, keeping both conduct and inquiry on a steadier course.

slides.title: Following the Judicious
bullets: I was convinced that I could not do better than follow the opinions of the most judicious while subjecting my own to examination.
I took cognizance of what they practised rather than what they said, for few speak exactly as they believe.

By watching what the judicious actually did in daily affairs rather than trusting their public declarations, the hidden inconsistencies between word and deed became impossible to ignore, sharpening the resolve to test every inherited opinion against clear reason alone.

This measured reliance on others therefore remained only a temporary scaffold, allowing one to move through ordinary life without paralysis while the work of radical examination proceeded in private, until each belief could stand or fall on its own evidence.

slides.title: Resolving to Change Nothing in Desires
bullets: My third maxim was to endeavor always to conquer myself rather than fortune, and to change my desires rather than the order of the world.
I accustomed myself to believe that nothing was entirely in my power except my thoughts, so that when I had done my best the lack of success could not make me unhappy.
This resolve turns every external setback into an occasion for strengthening the will, because desire now reaches only as far as my own judgments can reach.
The result is a steady inner calm that survives the most radical doubt, since nothing outside my thoughts retains the power to disturb the mind.

slides.title: Reviewing All Knowledge as Uncertain
bullets: I came to the conclusion that I might with freedom set about ridding myself of what remained of my opinions.
I made it my business in each matter to reflect particularly upon what might fairly be doubted and prove a source of error.

This sweeping review forces even the most familiar convictions, such as the testimony of the senses or the certainty of simple calculations, to stand trial without favor, revealing how easily custom alone has sustained them.

The immediate consequence is a temporary solitude of belief in which no claim retains authority until it earns it anew, exposing the risk that unexamined habits of thought will otherwise dictate what passes for truth.
slides.notes: Nine years of travel and doubt.

slides.title: Not Imitating the Sceptics
bullets: I did not imitate the sceptics who doubt only that they may doubt and seek nothing beyond uncertainty itself.
My design was to find ground of assurance, and I was successful enough in reaching conclusions of adequate certainty.

By turning doubt into a directed instrument rather than an end in itself, I cleared away every opinion that could be shaken and thereby prepared the ground for judgments that reason itself could certify.

The result is that methodical doubt, guided by the rules of clear and orderly reasoning, yields a foundation stable enough to support further knowledge without the endless drift that pure skepticism produces.

slides.title: The Principle of Clear and Distinct Conception
bullets: All the things which we clearly and distinctly conceive are true is certain only because God is or exists and is a Perfect Being.
Our ideas which to the extent of their clearness and distinctness are real and proceed from God must to that extent be true.
Once divine veracity secures our clear and distinct conceptions, even the most radical doubts raised by dreaming or an evil deceiver lose their force against ideas such as extension or number.
Consequently, any judgment that ventures beyond the limits of what is clearly conceived risks error, since only the divine guarantee, not the senses themselves, underwrites truth.

slides.title: Metaphysical Certitude Over Moral Assurance
bullets: Although we have a moral assurance of having a body and that stars and earth exist, no one can deny that there is sufficient reason to exclude entire assurance when the question relates to metaphysical certitude.
When asleep we can imagine ourselves possessed of another body and see other stars, so how do we know waking thoughts are not false?

This distinction compels reason to apply stricter scrutiny than everyday conduct requires, because any belief that can be shaken by the mere possibility of dreaming loses its claim to absolute standing.

The resulting tension drives us to test every conviction against the standard of indubitability, revealing that practical reliance on the senses must yield whenever metaphysical certainty is at stake.

slides.title: God Required to Remove Doubt
bullets: Men of the highest genius will not be able to give any reason sufficient to remove doubt of waking versus dreaming unless they presuppose the existence of God.
Even the principle that all things clearly and distinctly conceived are true is certain only because God exists.

Without that divine anchor the distinction between waking life and dream collapses into permanent suspicion, because nothing inside the mind itself can certify that its contents correspond to anything beyond it.
Consequently every clear and distinct perception, however compelling at the moment, loses its claim to lasting truth and reverts to the status of a possibly fabricated impression.
